notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Based on your memory, you're a self-employed UX consultant filing Schedule C with uneven income and no employer benefits — so tracking deductions carefully is especially valuable for you. Since tax matters involve specialized expertise, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor or CPA for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Here's a practical breakdown for June 2026:
Solid, well-established deductions
These are the categories the IRS regularly accepts for consultants with proper documentation:
Home office (if you have a dedicated workspace): You can deduct the business-use percentage of rent/mortgage interest, utilities, renters/homeowners insurance, HOA fees, repairs, and even housekeeping — based on square footage of your dedicated office divided by total home square footage. Alternatively, the simplified method gives you $5 per square foot (up to 300 sq ft, so a max of $1,500/year). The space must be used exclusively and regularly for business — a shared dining table doesn't qualify.
Health insurance premiums: As a self-employed individual purchasing marketplace coverage, your premiums are typically deductible as an above-the-line adjustment to income (not on Schedule C, but still reduces your AGI). This is one of the most valuable deductions in your situation.
Software and subscriptions: Design tools, project management apps, cloud storage, and any SaaS tools used for client work are generally straightforward deductions.
Professional development: Courses, books, and industry memberships directly related to UX consulting are well-supported.
Business phone and internet: The business-use percentage of these bills is deductible — not the full amount unless you have a dedicated business-only line.
Contractor and freelance payments: If you pay other freelancers to assist on projects, those are deductible (and may require a 1099-NEC if you pay a single person $600+ in a year).
Business banking and card fees: Your Bluevine and Amex Blue Business Plus fees, if any, are clean deductions.
Moderate-risk deductions (document carefully)
Meals: 50% of the cost of meals with clients or prospects is deductible — but you need to document who you met with and the business purpose. Meals eaten alone while working are generally not deductible.
Equipment and tech purchases: A new monitor, tablet, or laptop bought this month is deductible, but if it's also used personally, only the business-use percentage applies. Keep your receipts and note the primary use.
Travel: Airfare, hotels, and ground transport for client visits are deductible. Mixing personal travel with business travel requires careful allocation.
Higher-risk areas (proceed cautiously)
Vehicle expenses: If you drive to client meetings, you can deduct mileage (67 cents/mile in 2025; 2026 rate typically similar) or actual expenses. However, the IRS scrutinizes vehicle deductions heavily — a mileage log with dates, destinations, and business purposes is non-negotiable.
Entertainment: As of current tax law, entertainment expenses (sporting events, concerts, etc., even with clients) are 0% deductible. Don't confuse these with meals.
Excessive "mixed use" deductions: Deducting a personal vacation as a business trip, or your full phone bill when you use it mostly personally, is a common audit trigger. The IRS looks for consistency and proportionality.
Startup or exploratory costs: If you're evaluating expanding into a new service line this month, those investigatory costs (travel, research) may be deductible, but costs tied to a specific new engagement or contract may need to be capitalized. This is a nuanced area worth flagging for your CPA.
Practical action for this month
Given your preference for low-admin systems, the simplest habit is to run all business spending through your dedicated business accounts (Bluevine and Amex Blue Business Plus) and keep a brief note on any meal or travel receipt. That separation alone dramatically simplifies your Schedule C at year-end and reduces audit exposure.
Would you like help thinking through how to set aside an estimated tax reserve from your June income alongside tracking these deductions?
Note: Calculations, potential savings, and eligibility determinations are estimations for illustrative purposes only. Tax laws change frequently. Consult a qualified tax professional before making decisions.
